items.q: What does specific latent heat measure?
items.correct: The energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature.
items.misconception: The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 degree.
items.random_wrong: The temperature at which a substance melts or boils.
items.why: Students confuse specific latent heat with specific heat capacity because both involve 'heat' and 'kg', missing the no-temperature-change aspect of latent heat.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.
The answer examiners want is: kg/m^3. “kg/m^2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's constant. “rises” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Density of 500 g object with V = 250 cm^3 (g/cm^3)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
